Hey Ted — figured I'd give you a heads up that Ojipoji is live now for real: https://ojipoji.com   There's 90 days of game sets, at least for now
This feedback is great, thanks a ton! Obviously would love anything else you want to share as you play through it (but obviously we're all busy, so no worries) 👍

Awesome, thanks! Points are actually deprecated (which is why they're only left in the game set selector). I opted to go with a Wordle/Connections style "skill percentage" rather than straight points (for a host of boring reasons). So, it's more about chasing higher percentages than "6 or 8" points. The scorecard still has the number of questions you got right, though.  Did the crossword-style count not show up for the "jewels" one? I added those counts as a clue (because you're right, "gems" or "jewels" or "precious things" are all semantically correct)... I also phrased the theme guess as a rhyming riddle for that reason (but that rhyming element may not be obvious and isn't explained anywhere right now).  Ojipoji is a riff on "hodgepodge", yeah. Like if you were speaking fake Japanese. I tried a lot of names over the years (this idea has been brewing a while, long before a daily game version), and "Ojipoji" had the right blend of direction, playfulness, and distinctiveness.

Some assorted disconnected thoughts:  -Generally like it as a fun, daily trivia game! -Only played three games on mobile so far, haven't tried on PC.  -you mention points, but I don't see any overall score, or the different assignment of points by question.  -Ah, scratch that, I see them in the pink pill for game choices. But may be nice to have a dedicated or easier-to-find stats page, and a clear indication of which question is worth how many # of points. -For one of the themes I got "incorrect", I guessed "gems" when the answer was "jewels", which was frustrating to me as a user. One potential remedy to this is from another puzzle with a theme I got correct, "Chinese Dynasties". It let me know there were (7,9) letters, which was helpful. Having this crossword-style count could have forced me into "jewels". -What is "ojipoji"? A twist on "hodgepodge"?

Hey, so here's the prototype for my game: https://www.ojipoji.com.   I still need to write a "how to play" page, but it's basically this: Each day, there are three questions from different categories. There's also a secret "theme" linking them. Like any trivia, you answer the questions and guess the theme correctly.   The game is in test mode, so you can select any of the available game sets from the pink date pill at the top of the page.  Scoring rules put higher value on harder questions (i.e., guessing the theme without any answers revealed, then third question, then second, the first).  Would love to hear what you think is good, what sucks, what's fun, what's annoying — either from a content or a UX perspective (or, really, whatever you've got to say). Thanks a million, and hit me up if you have any questions!

1. What do you think is unique about your situation that made it possible for you to be successful?  I write really really fast, and I'm versatile on lots of different subject matter. Not every freelance writer can do this, and so they get bogged down with too many low-paying gigs for $0.02 per word, or (in my humble opinion) marketing themselves too narrowly as "only" being a freelance writer for obscure niche industries. I try to do a little of everything! Mostly within the realms of "tech" and "finance." But I'd like to get into more "ghostwriting" projects too -- I do a lot of that work behind the scenes for PR firms and book publishers. Trying to work my way up to doing my first book ghostwriting deal, as apparently that is a great line of work and could be a logical "next level" for my writing career.   I also just love the process of pitching story ideas. I like to be in dialogue with the internet. I like to cover breaking news. I like to follow unique trends and find ways to tie different storylines together. I have a restless mind which makes me ill-suited for traditional corporate cubicle life, but if I can just go hang out on the internet and get editors to approve my story ideas, I'm a happy camper.   2. What have been the biggest setbacks so far? What would you consider to be the biggest headache on a day to day basis?  Biggest headache: American health insurance! If you have a spouse with good health insurance, that is fantastic. Having self-employed health insurance all these years has been stressful and costly, but I've managed to just keep paying and paying for it.   Biggest setback: I lost a big, steady client in Dec. 2019 that had been with me for like 7 years and made up like 40% of my income. It was really stressful! And then in March 2020 the pandemic happened and the world ended. But I fortunately had a good base of other clients and I got referrals and found some new gigs on LinkedIn...as a freelancer, you just always have to be creating your own forward momentum, pitching yourself to new clients, introducing yourself to new opportunities, trying out for new things. Can't spend too much time waiting around or feeling despondent, haha. Which I also am vulnerable to doing! I like to be "slightly too busy" at all times, and when that doesn't happen or a project gets stalled or a client doesn't get back to me, it can be a little lonely. But there's always more work! Just have to go find it sometimes.   And at the moment, I'm busier than ever before and happier as a freelancer than ever before, and hopefully I'll stay this busy for a little while longer. But in case I lose another big project, I know what I need to do to go find another one. (I get so swamped with work that I stop applying for more work, you know? That's the constant battle.)   Hope this helps! Good luck w/ everything. It really is possible, you just have to revamp your mindset a little and go after "lots of smaller projects and client relationships" instead of "one big full-time paycheck." But sometimes, employers are more willing to hire people on a "small" flexible basis! It can be a win-win!   Oh I would also say: make sure you're charging enough per hour/per project. You need to charge MORE than you think, per billable hour. I'm of the opinion that "minimum wage" for freelancers should be $50-$75 per billable hour, haha. Not every project pays that! But a rule of thumb is, whatever your hourly "wage" from a full-time salary would be, triple it. Clients will often pay more than you think; they get the benefit of "low overhead, no payroll taxes, etc." and so you can often make more per hour as a freelancer than you would as an employee. And then just keep juggling all the deadlines and keep everyone happy, and watch the money roll in!   -- BG

Hi Devon! I'm sorry to hear about the layoff. It seems like tech startups are getting less and less stable as long-term employers these days; seeing it happen to a few good folks in my network.   Freelancing! I love it! I never want to do anything else ever again. I worked at a tech startup for about a year in 2021-2022, but other than that I've been freelancing since 2009 and freelancing full time mostly since 2010. It suits the way my mind works; apparently I'd rather juggle lots of smaller projects at once, than think about one "big" job. (Of course, most jobs also require juggling lots of smaller stuff at once, haha. But with more Zoom calls!)   I recommend signing up for Kaitlyn Arford's Freelance Opportunities newsletter -- this is curated and well-managed, and most opportunities are good paying. Do you want to do writing, editing, marketing consulting? Her newsletter has a little of everything and can be a good place to start.   I also recommend signing up for LinkedIn Premium. Change the way you use your LinkedIn from searching for full-time jobs to searching for freelance/contract gigs. I got a really great gig on LinkedIn that I found out about through my daily "freelance writer" search term updates.   There are a few gig platforms/freelance job sites that might be helpful too, but I haven't used any in awhile. Upwork is where I got my start back in 2009; there might be some good gigs on there, but also lots of low-paying gigs. Still could be worth a look. FlexJobs has remote/hybrid/flexible work opportunities, and so does Indeed. I haven't used Fiverr or Freelancer.com myself, but those are two other sites that get decent reviews.   Also if you want to do content marketing (writing or editing), check out Contently and SkyWord. I used to get lots of good paying gigs from both of those, but it can take time to get approved and start getting assignments; much of it is out of your control, just based on which projects they have that are a good fit at any given time.   I hope this helps! I love the freelance life, and it's been a marvelous adventure. This year I've traveled to Germany and New Mexico for weeks at a time without having to take PTO or ask a boss for permission. And I realize not everyone is lucky enough to make a good living at it like I do; but I also feel like, for people who have the right skills, demeanor, and "hustle," you can make more money as a freelancer than you can at a full-time job -- and without the full-time job stresses and weird hassles and layoffs. I sometimes lose clients as a freelancer, but no single client can ever "lay me off" and take away 100% of my income at a moment's notice. As a freelancer, I feel resilient and well-established and well-connected! And I never set an alarm clock, haha. It's like being semi-retired.   I hope this helps! Good luck with everything and let me know if you have other questions.   -- Ben Gran

SUBJECT: Marketing & sales data… finally playing nice.
CONTENT: Hey there Devon,    I’m Eric, founder of Software Prophets. We help marketing and sales leaders clean and connect their messy data (from CRMs, MAPs, ads, sales tools, etc.) so they can finally see what’s really working and what’s not.    We do this by quietly connecting and cleaning the messy data hiding in your CRM, MAP, ad tools, and sales platforms. No new tools. No big IT projects. Just smarter decisions, made faster.    Want to trade calendars or I can send mine?    Thanks,  Eric Lentz  Founder, Software Prophets  eric.lentz@softwareprophets.com  softwareprophets.com/schedule    P.S. Here’s what it looks like in practice:    Imagine a mid-size SaaS company scaling fast but struggling to track customer behavior from first ad click to closed deal.    Their data lives across:  • Salesforce  • HubSpot  • LinkedIn Ads  • Outreach  • Web analytics & email platforms    The challenges are familiar:  • MQLs that don’t sync properly  • Sales flying blind before conversations  • Attribution reports that never quite add up    But by cleaning up records, connecting systems, and creating a unified, real-time view of the customer journey, here’s what becomes possible:  • Revenue attributed clearly to campaigns  • High-value accounts identified earlier  • Reports built in minutes, not hours    Is this the kind of visibility your team’s aiming for?

Its true a lot of these came in after, so my experience is really through side projects + using them (or trying to!) in a more corporate setting. Happy either way, I'll let you decide if that is useful. For what it's worth my findings can be summarised quite quickly: a) project with larger org - they dictate what is and isn't possible. b) project with smaller org - they either dont care/understand the implications or they just say no. So its not so much about the tools, more about the contractual restrictions and limitations on sharing code with LLMs. So best for me would be to run my LLM locally or in a private setup but I dont have the volume of work to warrant it.

Thanks so much for connecting, Devon!    My top strategy to boost quality content traffic is simple: Put your expertise first.    Not your product, not SEO keywords, not ChaptGPT prompts, and not the "top 5 industry trends."    I found that in-depth interviews with internal stakeholders (I know, I know, easier said than done) allows companies to bypass overdone content ideas (i.e. do we need one more article on "what is X insurance" or "what is AP?") and cut directly to present and future search inquiries.    While evergreen content is essential, relevancy is the top differentiator. Focusing on expertise first and fitting in SEO during the editing process is far more effective, in my experience, in driving quality traffic and building customer engagement.    Happy to discuss more content strategies for long-term growth in a quick call. If that works, just pick  whatever time works best for you here: https://calendly.com/kelseycopywriter/copywriting-discovery?back=1

Hey Devon!   I enjoyed chatting with you, and always happy to help anyone considering freelancing!  Totally understand the worry about uncertainty, particularly with health issues. My rate calculation included health insurance and medical bills. I don't mind sharing!   I've been really pleasantly surprised with the insurance from The Marketplace. There were lots of options to choose from, with varying premiums, deductibles, etc. I got a BCBS policy that's much better than what I had through my employer, and I've never had a problem using it. Pre-authorizations have been fast, and I've never been surprised with a bill I wasn't expecting. My premium is $1014/month for coverage for myself and my spouse, with zero deductible and no copay or coinsurance, just flat-prices for services (for example, a primary care visit is $45, a specialist visit is $90, an MRI is $400, etc.). And customer service has been fantastic — I've called with coverage questions several times.  But every state has its own thing going on within the Marketplace, so you'll want to see what's available to you. I found The Marketplace really easy to use, although it can be difficult to parse the options, with so many variables (premiums, deductibles, copays, coinsurances, etc.) You can fill out your information to see what's available before signing up so you could get some peace of mind for if you had to go that route.  A really nice aspect of The Marketplace is that the pricing is based on your income — so if you're out of work, or if you can't get clients, your premiums will be way lower.  I will add a caution here — this is all based on the program and law as they stand now. The Rs have been talking about nixing the program and/or eliminating the subsidies. It's hard to predict what will happen to the program long term. Prior to Obamacare, insurance companies could refuse to cover preexisting conditions. (I don't know how people avoided going bankrupt back then.)   But I'm of the mind that nothing is ever truly guaranteed, things are always changing, and it's always worth betting on yourself! We can figure things out when the next change comes. 😌

I'm so sorry to hear it, Devon! Tech is absolutely bonkers right now. That's good that you are doing ok in spite of it.   Absolutely. I decided to go out on my own after a layoff — I realized that job security is nonexistent, so I might as well take control of my own destiny. :) I've never regretted it. I make way more money, work 30 hours a week, and get to choose what projects I work on.   Of course, during crazy times like what's happening now, it's harder to find work. But that's reflected in the larger job market in tech so I don't think it's any harder to find freelance work than full-time right now.   The biggest thing you need for success is a good network — you want most of your clients coming via referrals. I built my network from nothing, so it's totally doable. This book has a great approach to getting referrals: https://www.amazon.com/dp/1683509269  I also use cold outreach (like I did with you). About 30% of my clients come from cold outreach. It will be easier if you specialize in a particular type of work or a specific industry to differentiate yourself (I'm about to niche down, myself.)  Beyond that, it's just basic business acumen. Ed Gandia has some fantastic resources for packaging, pricing, project management, client management, etc. https://b2blauncher.com/  I'm happy to do a call if it would be helpful!

I was single so no backup income. I was working for some startups early in my career and I saw how easily and quickly they could fold. I felt like I could depend upon myself to pull in what I needed instead of getting blindsided. That, and I was getting good signal that people wanted my work. I had started doing freelance before I went for it full time (because the startup I was at went a different direction and I was laid off). So at that point it was the easiest way for me to regroup and just start taking on more. One of the best things I did was to get hooked up with a small agency that was looking to outsource work. They would get the clients and deal with the meetings and money and farm it to me. Of course you don’t earn big, but it lowered the administrative load at the beginning when I was just starting out my book of business and every client meeting would have been a time suck that was likely unpaid.

Heh, having worked with you for several months at least, I can see how your hustle might lead in that direction :) Good to know though — it seems like one of the trickier parts is managing the workload, since you're not guaranteed a paycheck on a regular basis. Can I ask if you were single at the time? One of the advantages I've got is that my wife's job covers most of our expenses, and it's fairly stable (law).  Cool that you didn't have a hard time finding clients. I've got decent relationships with a few people in my network, although I haven't really tested it out. I expect I could get a few contracts in that area. If I didn't, I'd have to seriously rethink it.  Can I ask what made you go the self-employment route in the first place? I'm curious how you evaluated the opportunity there and decided to go in that direction. I'm also wondering what factors of your situation may have contributed to your success (before you finally went full time at Strive).

I did. A number of years before striveworks, I was doing that full time and then I started just doing a few odd jobs for trusted customers, though I have had to stop in the last two years. It was rewarding to get paid on hustle alone, but I honestly made myself sick by taking on more than I could handle at the time because I didn’t have a ton of retainers and I didn’t know if the next month would be feast or famine. It ended up being a relief at the end to go back working for someone else. I might choose to do things differently and in a healthier way if I did it again. On a positive note, I didn’t have to invest much time into finding clients due to my network at the time. So that is something to consider… can you tap a network for some quick wins or will you be competing with others on a digital marketplace.

any who yes before jumping in with an early start up, get the clearest picture you can get on the go to market strategy, sign, and NDS and look at their strategy and their stated differentiation. When I was your age, I'm pretty sure I could not have spotted it but now I feel like I've grown that muscle and I could've grown it earlier if I knew what I was looking for. But I really encourage you as a content person to get that differentiation story told to you. I can't remember if I told you, but I was tech writer, and I spent a lot of time with my head down in the content weeds and I didn't spend enough time actually looking at the value proposition of the product or platform I was writing about. And that was a failing of mine. I won't repeat.

I remember thinking that Jim actually seemed to be actively resisting any movement toward a private sector strategy that had differentiation grounded in lineage , monitoring or remediation. And that just made no sense to me and it drove other people crazy too. Interestingly those people not there anymore either and I begin to wonder if I drove a little too crazy with my constant questions about it, but that's in my past I have moved on.

The other painful throwaway that never made sense to me is the patent that Striveworks got that would've really served the lineage story. Lineage is absolutely critical and so many domains like Fintech from which Jim himself came. It never made any sense to me that we just put that patent on the shelf right after it was issued. The stack moved away from making use of it and I had a meeting with. matt after the patent was issued I was super excited for it, and he seemed very discouraged to me that there seemed no plan to implement any of it, which is another one of those moments of strive I wondered what's going on here are we a shell cover or something else

Two doesn't make a pattern, but it certainly makes an observation doesn't it and 11 months it would be interesting to correlate that time against the age of the Company in each of your cases. not that you asked, but in strives case from my point of view there go to market strategy was nonexistent because Jim did not seem willing to get pointy about it. A number of us were looking at what Eric K was doing from day one and saying he's onto something: monitoring and remediation is real. I know because I worked directly in that space and it looked like we had some thing differentiated, but then I saw how Eric was getting so few followers on his open source GIT project. And I recently learned from my Stent and helping some good friends who are new founders who have decades experience in Silicon Valley and they're doing a startup to build GenAI agents and going the open source route initially, I learned that getting stars on a project is critical for VCs. if you're not getting traffic expressed through stars to like the project then you're not considered to be making progress. That's a recent learning of mine. It seems so silly but VC's care because I went pitching with this group.

Ha! All good — and thanks for that detailed answer. Yeah, I'm certainly re-evaluating options now, as this was second RIF from an earlyish stage startup in two years, at 11 months each (which is a pattern I can't help but notice). I really appreciate having your thoughts here, since you've been around Silicon Valley for a while. I feel like you're probably right, with companies constantly trimming as an ongoing rule (at least until interest rates come down and there's looser reins on VC investment).  My thinking here is a combination of factors. While GenAI isn't going to do everything in marketing anytime soon, I can see a lot of the content function getting automated/rolled into other roles (i.e., product marketing, demand generation, etc.) It has me wondering if it's smarter to shift my focus from being the guy who can write stuff toward another function. I do appreciate the startup environment, where I get a broader range of responsibilities (at Strive and other companies, I've dipped into those other disciplines, which I appreciate. I like the variety.)  Tech still makes sense to me (as much as anything): It's energizing and I understand the broad strokes of how it works, so I'm still inclined toward that versus, say, healthcare or finance or something. I'm actually starting to talk to people who've gone independent as freelancers or starting agencies to see if that may be a worthwhile fit (although that obviously has its own pitfalls or consider).

Sorry that was a long dictated. Answer to my phone as I run to grab lunch with my son, but I'm more than delighted to chitchat if it will help because these things are really important, our careers are how we spend most of our lives.

oh my gosh, you're having a career existential crisis! This is terrific news! Most people never have them and then wake up 20 years later and a job they despise feeling they wasted their life and then realize perhaps it's just better to keep their heads down trudging forward until they finally retire and then ultimately die. Sorry, don't mean to be so grim. Can you tell I am a serial startup junkie? so yes ups and downs. I mean people say oh we've never seen a market like this before and that seems to go with every up-and-down you get weird curveballs like Covid obviously no one saw that but then we also had the freak economics effect of all the hiring because of Covid. Like who saw that coming? and now tech companies seem to be adopting a new strategy of constantly trimming kind of like a jet in flight, looking for efficiencies all throughout the flight, changing out Eudes and trimming the flaps to optimize for efficiency. So I think general layoffs are going to become the new norm just to gut feeling, they will be little ones but they will be trimming. I think that's for the larger more established start up certainly with the public ones that are having to answer to the street. as for true text startups, they are always going to need people. Jen can begin the lift, but human intelligence will always be required especially if the startup is in a brand new space i.e. there's literally no content that's been created for it yet I think perhaps framework for you too address your crisis if you have not already considered this is understanding where you think you are most comfortable early startups more established ones or just huge companies like nationwide in Columbus. But be aware that none of what I've just described is now free from RIFs. there was a time when going to nationwide would've been the safest bet for the rest of your life. They used to be a customer of mine two companies ago and they had horrible layoffs to optimize for efficiencies just like tiny startups so if you take that as a rule of thumb, and it's a risk anywhere you go you might as well aim for the environment that you like most. Wide range of responsibilities and offer you a lot of opportunity to innovate but some people don't like that some people want prescribed waterfall list of work in which case go to a large companyif you're considering the future of your life, which is basically what you're considering when you think about a career aim for a place where you think you will be socially and emotionally happy and the work will satisfy you. Even when you're laid off again because it will happen again you'll have no regrets.

Great to hear you landed somewhere you're feeling good about!  I'm curious about the DR job, but I actually think it's best for me to take a step back and reassess what I'm doing here on out. Really appreciate it — and I'd love to hear your thoughts, as an industry veteran, on weathering the ups and downs that inevitably come with tech cycles.

CONTENT: Good morning,  My name is Stella Lyubarsky and I am a Recruiting Manager with Robert Half.  I am reaching out with an exciting opportunity for your review and consideration.  I am including below the description of the role. If you are interested, please respond to me at your earliest convenience with your resume and availability for a call.   Benefits:   o	Medical, Dental, Vision (82% covered)  	Signa  Gold, Silver, Bronze o	Long term Care, LTD, STD and Life Insurance (100% covered)  o	Vacation:  3 weeks, Standard holidays and 2 floating holidays  o	401K match – None currently.   o	100% Remote work model   -	Salary: $50,000  About the company:  With more than 2,000 customers and operations in 13 cities around the world, we are the AI technology solutions provider-of-choice to 4 out of 5 of the world’s biggest technology companies, as well as leading companies across financial services, insurance, technology, law, and medicine. Heh, I just wrapped up at a startup where things changed constantly. It didn't really bother me — all part of the ultimate goal, y'know. My last job was highly focused on safety for utility workers and how the software pertained to it. So, I had to get up to speed on concepts in industrial safety fast (some of which I already knew), as well as how the utility industry works, and how Urbint's AI tech worked (for writing mid-funnel technical explainers). Interesting stuff, and I learned a lot.   Hmm, it's certainly not going to hurt to throw my hat in the ring. Should I go through the website?

All in all, as long as you don't get bothered by things changing frequently, then it should be okay. But if that sounds stressful to you, give it some thought. Sometimes we change courses on what we're doing and then realize it didn't work and we change back, or change again. It is kinda tiring, but our core values are adaptability, iteration, impact, and fun, so it kinda comes with the territory. I just try and roll with it as much as I can!

The Technical Writer role is actually on my team though. It involves writing software reviews, like what I do, for People Managing People (and other sites) but that role is focused on writing for ALL our sites, so you need to be able to jump in and out of different types of software. Which I initially found hard, but after 1.5 years, I've gotten way better at it. Everything has a learning curve! Was your most recent role very technical in nature too?

BWZ is laser-focused on scaling exponentially, and sometimes I think it's a little unrealistic, but that's still the end goal. We currently run about 10 sites, and are launching 4 new ones this year (including the CTO). That role reports to a different manager, who is really nice, but things tend to change quickly around here so hectic is a good word for it. It's not DTP hectic (no OT required and we have flexible hours) but we do have a lot of specific metrics and KPIs that we need to meet. My job is focused on inventory content (which we monetize, so they scrutinize it WAY more) but the CTO is brand new so it's a long way away from being monetizable. That role is more focused on brand building, but it sounds like it will also involve managing our QA Lead website, which is VERY technical. and that site IS currently monetized. Hi,  My name is Monalisa Mohapatra and I am a Technical Recruiter at Collabera. I saw your profile on one of the job board and felt you would be an excellent fit for this opportunity. If you are available for a New Project/Job, please send me your word formatted resume to Monalisa.Mohapatra@collabera.com   or feel free to contact me at 973.889.5265 to discuss as soon as possible.  Job Title:                             Technical Writer II Duration:                             6 to 11   months Location:                             Seattle, WA  Larger team is Amazon Lumberyards, package of various software tools that helps game developers make games. Customer facing documentation by technical writers - customers are the people making games. Tickets are for documentation covering various topics. Game engines are very complex software used by designers, artists, programmers. Basically a big tool with a bunch of smaller tools inside of it. Outstanding tickets are areas that haven't been documented. Ex: there is a spelling error on this page, or it can be larger, ex: particle editor needs to be documented. Team is trained and familiar so they take a day and half on average per ticket. Several hundred engineers writing software, documentation team was understaffed, can’t keep up with new engineering work that was happening.  Key projects: Clear backlog. Chunks of tickets coalesce around a feature area, so the contractor will learn one without much randomization. Get familiar with one area of the engine and work around area. Work environment: Pretty flexible. Come in around 10-7 or 8. Early starters are fine too. Other people on team will be there starting around 8:30-9. A period of collaboration with people who wrote the software, get trained on it and then it’s head down while you’re doing the writing. A little more collaboration while doing a technical review. Writing team are fairly introverted, talk one on one, not noisy and not a ton of conversations. No full time remote, but once they get their feet under them, they can work from home one day per week.  Need to be on site while they learn. Once they’re trained, expectation is averaging a day and a half per ticket. After an initial month, they’ll be looking to have contract writers hitting something near that. If they’re not getting close to that, it won’t garner an extension.  FYI: A lot of times, contractors are called in when things are on fire. This is a different scenario, the tickets they have are not ones that nobody wanted, it’s real, actual valuable work.   Experience/Skills Needed: Must have done documentation for a significant technical software product!! Ownership is pretty good indicator.  Writers just document the stuff they’ve been told to document, but those who excel can also work in other documentation and see improvement spot, take initiative and make a change. If something has been presented in a certain way, and it’s overly complicated to write about, maybe there’s a better way to implement, so a good tech writer will let the engineers know. Dive Deep is important, have to dive into technical space and deeply understand before they write about it.  Automatic DQ: If they've never done documentation of a significantly technical software product, they need to see a history of that. Writers doing end user facing, IU that were fairly simple. This is technical software for developers who are technical people. If you see work where they’ve done developer facing documentation.  Services To Be Performed The key responsibility for the technical writer is to independently develop and deliver documentation for core game engine technologies and tools.  Required skills A minimum of 5 years in a technical writing role Proven experience designing and delivering customer-oriented documentation Strong written and verbal communication skills Experience working directly with development teams  Preferred skills Experience with cloud services or game technology Experience with instructional design Experience with an XML-based authoring system Previous experience working in an agile environment Passion to thrive in a fast-paced, ever-changing environment Proven experience using and following the Microsoft Manual of Style for Technical Publications (MSTP) A proactive and open-minded attitude to resolving problems and delivering results   Monalisa Mohapatra Technical Recruiter 973 889 5265 Monalisa.Mohapatra@Collabera.com

As a quick synopsis, I'm Devon Wells:  - I'm an experienced copywriter and content marketer based in Seattle (four years in marketing, several more in media).  - I've developed and led the multi-channel content strategy for Hitachi Construction Machinery's fleet management division since 2014.  - My work has contributed to exponential jumps in inbound leads, social shares, website traffic, and other KPIs for my employers.  - I'm an avid cook and I'm supremely excited about the prospect of creating content to promote the Joule sous vide device.   For these reasons (and the rest detailed in my original résumé and cover letter), I think I'd be an ideal fit for the position. In fact, I've actually started working up some sample content for the Joule based around this customer persona I created (https://drive.google.com/open?id=1qk7RzENFWociiejvjXHHfUrbqy3XPNxN).   I'd love to get together with you to discuss the position further, so please let me know if you're still looking for someone to help with ChefSteps' marketing needs. Thanks for your time and consideration!  Cheers,  Devon

Hi Amy,    Sorry for taking so long to get back to you — things have been a little crazy around here.     Great to hear you’ve been enjoying Tianjin (and that I was some help in that regard)! I confess, I’m a little surprised. Almost everyone I know digs Japan more than China. It’s true, though — the people I encountered were overwhelmingly easygoing and welcoming and eager to chat about anything. Funny you compare it to Richmond — I work out there now and I’ve been seeking out the funky Chinese restaurants and shops whenever I get the chance. While it’s not totally the same, it’s nice being able to jog my memories with a 15-minute SkyTrain ride. (I do miss my friends that are still in Dalian though, but I’ll get back there some day.)    It sounds like you dug up lots of cool work to keep you busy. It goes to show — there’s always stuff around if you know where to look. Calgary should be fun! Have you been there before? Whenever I get over the mountains I’m heading to Toronto or Newfoundland, but I figure I’ll have a reason to swing through Cowtown one of these days. Shame about the pollution and food safety issues in Tianjin though — it’s a problem that really dampens a place with so much to offer. But, there’s not much you can do when your family’s health is at stake.     Actually, I may be making a move myself. My girlfriend took a great job in Seattle a couple of months ago and it looks like I’ll be heading down myself in the near future. Sadly, it means heading back to technical writing for a while (since it’s a NAFTA-approved job that will get me a work visa). But, fingers crossed that a tech centre of that size has some hybrid content-technical positions that won’t be such a drag.     Glad to hear everything’s working out great. Stay in touch and we’ll talk soon!    Devon

Hi Devon,    It's great to hear from you and I'm so glad you're doing well and I was able to contribute in a small way. I think content writing is one of the best things to hit the world of business ever. Do you enjoy freelancing?    Thank you for your information on living here last year. It helped ease that secret voice inside that was wondering what the heck I was doing, while being gung ho on the outside.    Tianjin is a nice city- very modern and large. I live in a specialized economic development zone and people call it "China-lite". It actually looks a lot like Vancouver/Richmond.     I've had some very cool opportunities - I wrote a bunch of marketing materials for an American CEO (we met on LinkedIn - but we actually live in the same apartment complex -I knew his kids - in a city of 14 million people) and I'm writing for a US trade magazine plus writing some English lessons. .and was hired by an American CEO to write a bunch of marketing materials for renewable energy.     I am heading back to Canada though - to Calgary where my husband landed a teaching job for September. We're finding it to be too polluted here with a lot of food safety issues for our kids at school/daycare. I'm looking forward to going home but have enjoyed the people here. They are warm and friendly (and love kids) even if they shove you out of line and hork luggies on your shoes :). I enjoy it 10X more than Japan - people here are more relaxed and seem to enjoy what they have more. Less tension, working hours and so on.    Do you miss it?

SUBJECT: RE: Looking to connect
CONTENT: Not sure this will help, but we look for a number of things when screening writing candidates:     --Process. We want writers who acknowledge, evangelize for, and practice iterative writing / content creation. It's cyclical, social, and usually non-linear.    --Design sensibilities. On our UX team, we need people how understand and, ideally, speak the language of design, as well- or nebulously defined as that can be. Functional, sensible document design is an obvious must, but we also need writers who understand design process (specifically in software) and communicate effectively with our design team. That requirement obviously changes if you go work with devs or some people in some other domain.    --Basic technical writing chops. You know that most decent professional writing starts with *some* form of telling readers what your about to tell them, actually telling them, and then telling them what you just told them.    --Rhetorical awareness. You're obsessed with knowing your audience and their needs. You have to have a purpose for your writing. You rely on established genres before even thinking about trying to carve out a new one. You are always aware of your tone.     --Excellence in feedback. You're constructive and detail-oriented. You know how to persuade others you are right, and you also know how to accept that someone else might have a better idea. You also know when to focus on mechanics and when to focus on the big idea.     There are others, but these are the big ones (off the top of my head). There are plenty of sites out there, too, that discuss the intersection of writing and design (if not exclusively). Smashing Magazine and A List Apart are two classics.     Hope that helps!    P

SUBJECT: RE: Looking for info about working at Amazon
CONTENT: Hi Devon,    Various Agile and Lean methodologies are applied where they make sense throughout Amazon.  Some examples: Software development teams often hold regular scrums, but most other teams do not. Amazon warehouse environments contain andon signal lights for various things.  I've recently started using a kanban board to track my tasks because the nature of my work doesn't fit more complex tools (like MS Project), but no one else in my group works this way.    Some teams use formal IA methods and tools (such as DITA, oXygen, MadCap Flare, etc.) I had little prior knowledge of these tools and I am still not using them. Instead, I'm hoping to provide an "interactive document portal" for my audiences via XWiki or SharePoint.  Wish me luck!    At this point, none of the writing tools or methods I use are proprietary. Interviewing, organizing, structuring, formatting, and clarifying consume the bulk of my writing and editing work.  Re-prioritizing and being flexible as to when and how information arrives are key.  Diagramming and data visualization skills also come in handy.    You might try contacting Luke Easterwood here on LinkedIn. I don't work with him, but it appears that his group works on customer-facing UI projects.    Best of luck!  ~Eric

SUBJECT: RE: Looking for info about working at Amazon
CONTENT: Hi Devon,    Your impressions of Amazon's TW positions are similar to those I had before being hired, and they turned out to be fairly accurate.   Many of the jobs in corporate Amazon are "hybrid" positions.  For example, the Project Managers I work with all have in-depth, hands-on computer networking experience--and they need it!      Folks here who write external-facing content often have associated UX or web development duties. While there are standards for external content, internal writers have to seek out (or create) their own standards and tools. This gives the job a bit of a "contractor" flavor.     Most writers do not belong to a dedicated TW department. As "lone writers" we figure out how to support our teams by connecting with other writers dispersed throughout the company.     I spend a lot of my time exploring IA approaches and doing developmental edits of internal material: white papers, SOPs, diagrams, etc. Although I'm seldom the content originator, my version typically becomes the document of record.      Sorry that I didn't reply to you earlier--I don't check my Linked-in messages very regularly.  Feel free to ask any other questions.  I will answer them to the best of my ability.    ~Eric

Hi Amy,    Wow, that does sound a lot like freelance journalism! Great to know that’s how the market’s going. I’ve done scattered pitches and introductions over the years (chiefly for newspapers and magazines), but I’ve backed off recently to concentrate on finding a full-time gig. If that’s how companies are seeking out content, though, maybe I should shoot for some freelance work — on the side, if nothing else.     As for technical writing, I’ve found it’s not really my thing. It’s a poor fit for my personality. Other technical writers I know love it, but they’re more into procedures and systems than I am; they’re also more conservative and quiet. It’s a stable field and there seems to be lots of work around, but prepare for a lot of boring tasks (with boring people) if you go down that road.    It probably depends on the industry, but in my experience most of the work involves confidential company reports, so you don’t get much of a portfolio to wave around either. Occasionally, we’ve gotten contracts that were more content than technical writing, which were fun, but the nuts-and-bolts of the job itself has been pretty dull (hence, I’m looking to branch out).    It’s a shame you’re not headed to Dalian anymore, as I know that city really well. I’ve never been to Tianjin, but it’s big enough to have some interesting stuff going on, no doubt. Unfortunately, I can’t help you much with the dog important situation — everyone I know with dogs in China got them in-country — but try those sites that Ty sent along; I’m sure they’ll turn up something. Regarding your question on content writing and freelancing  - there is a lot of need but you need to unearth it, send letters of introduction and so on. A lot of companies (Manulife for example, Telus, Canadian Tourism) build up a stable of freelancers to contribute regularly. You can look up other Vancouver freelance writers on LinkedIn or by portfolio, see where they write and pitch yourself if that's your thing, or through networking.    There are also a lot of online publications that do the same (Pivot Magazine) and so on. In this way, it's more like freelance writing. (For example, from ProBlogger I just hooked up with FitSMallBusiness.com a new site in New York and I write a weekly buyer's guide for their site. Hey dude!    Good to hear from you! What's brought you to the Pacific Northwest? (To be fair, I can't remember where in the US you were from in the first place, heh.) Vancouver's grand, but I think I prefer Portland based on my three days there (cities are always different when you live there, though.) I found the vibe to be less uppity and more DIY and with fewer silly regulations. No accounting for taste, though.    For a person interested in social enterprise, you're looking in the right place; Vancouver seems to take that sort of thing seriously. When you say "help youth realize their dreams", what do you have in mind? A lot of the social enterprise stuff I come across in Vancouver involves working with vulnerable people in the Downtown Eastside (the big depressed neighbourhood in Vancouver that marks Canada's poorest area code). For youth specifically, I'd have to do some thinking. I know a couple of journalists who write on stuff in that regard. Maybe get in touch with Katie Hyslop; we're old buds and she could likely tell you more than I could. (She's on LinkedIn for sure.)    Heads up (although I'm sure you've considered it): Moving here as an American can be a real bitch. My girlfriend's from Ohio and she's always falling out of status and waiting around for the incredibly sluggish Canadian government to approve her visa renewals. Annoying, but you dealt with biggest annoyances in China so I'm sure you're up for it.    All the best, man. Keep in touch!    Devon

Hi Devon,    My name is Matt Roberts and I am running for the MUN Board of Regents as an Alumni representative. Voting just began and runs until May 23 and anyone who has a degree or diploma from any Memorial campus can vote. The website for voting can be found here:     http://www.munalum.ca/Board-of-Regents/Board-of-Regents-Elections-Nominees/    I am excited to be involved in this process and believe I would be a positive addition to the Memorial Board of Regents. Below is a little bio about myself. Please don’t hesitate to ask any questions you may have and thanks for your consideration for this position.     Originally from St. John’s, Matt graduated from MUN in 2006 with a Bachelor of Commerce and then completed an MBA at Dalhousie University. In his current role as Vice President, Investment Banking at Clarus Securities in Toronto, he assists companies in raising equity and debt capital. In this capacity, Matt has begun to promote Newfoundland and Labrador business with a goal of bringing new investors and funds to the east coast. He believes that NL represents a significant opportunity and the level of entrepreneurship and technology being developed at Memorial is on par with the best universities in Canada. In July, 2013, Matt brought a group of potential clients from Toronto to St. John’s and met with 10 local companies over a two day period. The positive reception and high quality of meetings enforced the belief that Newfoundland is at the forefront of Canadian innovation and Matt intends to continue to bring new investors and clients to NL. If elected, he hopes to further promote Memorial University and be involved with maintaining MUN’s high level of quality and education. Besides his role at Clarus, Matt is also a volunteer with WWF Canada as part of the Climate and Energy Team as well as a CFA Charterholder.     Regards,     Matthew Roberts, MBA, CFA

It's been good for me in a lot of ways -- my job revolves around writing and editing at the moment, which is great; I quit smoking six months ago; I'm way healthier than I've ever been. Still, I can feel a sense of adventure ... not dying, but definitely straining to get out. Maybe I just need a vacation.    All in all, though, it's tip-top. You hear from Tom or Rick ever? CONVERSATION TITLE: Duke Chief Marketing Officer Program | 7 Months
CONTENT:
Hi Devon, 


The Duke Chief Marketing Officer Program (Duke CMO Program) from Duke University’s Fuqua School of Business is a 7-month multi-modular, immersive program designed for experienced CMOs and senior marketing professionals ready to leverage AI, data, and innovation to drive enterprise impact.
